sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Elbow pain
History of Present Illness
The patient reports elbow pain on the inside of his elbow, which began approximately four days ago. The pain is exacerbated by pottery creation, particularly when applying decorations, and radiates down his arm. He denies any recent injury. He has tried applying ice initially, but the pain persists. He reports waking up at night due to the pain. He has been taking ibuprofen, but it hasn't provided significant relief. The patient has a history of hypertension and diabetes, and is currently taking lisinopril 10mg daily and metformin 500mg twice daily. He admits to sometimes forgetting to take his medications and occasionally consuming sweets, leading to slightly elevated blood sugar readings. He also enjoys baking and making cinnamon rolls, and has a sweet tooth.
Findings

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Physical examination reveals moderate tenderness to palpation over the medial epicondyle of the right elbow. Resistance to pronation elicits pain. Wrist flexion and extension elicit pain. Pulse and blood pressure are normal. Lung sounds are clear bilaterally. The patient's oxygen saturation is 100%. The patient's vital signs are as follows: blood pressure 120/78, heart rate 18 beats per minute, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 100%. The patient's weight has increased by approximately five pounds since the last visit. The x-ray shows no fractures or bony abnormalities.
Assessment
The patient's symptoms are consistent with medial epicondylitis (tennis elbow). He also continues to have hypertension and diabetes mellitus.
Plan
Order a sling for the right arm to be worn during waking hours. Prescribe ibuprofen 600 mg every six hours as needed for pain. Instruct the patient to apply ice packs to the elbow for 20 minutes, three times daily. Schedule an appointment for a follow-up in two weeks to assess progress and adjust treatment as needed. Order an A1C test to assess long-term blood sugar control. Refer the patient to a registered dietitian for guidance on dietary management and portion control. Recommend a referral to a diabetic counseling service for further support and education. Schedule a follow-up appointment in four weeks if symptoms persist.
